planting.note: Plant in frost-free, humid tropical conditions with deep, moist, well-drained, slightly acidic soil; salak is a clumping spiny palm — plant male and female plants together for fruiting, or obtain a hermaphroditic variety.

directions: Plant in deep, moist, humus-rich, well-drained, slightly acidic soil in partial shade in frost-free tropical conditions; salak (snake fruit) is a dramatic spiny clustering palm from Indonesia and Malaysia producing clusters of pear-shaped fruits with distinctive reddish-brown scaly skin that closely resembles a snake's skin — hence the common name. The fruit has three lobes of crisp, dry, sweet-acidic flesh (texture somewhat like a dry apple) with a complex flavor variously described as sweet, astringent, honey-like, and reminiscent of cinnamon, varying considerably by variety. The most prized variety is 'Salak Bali' from Bali. High humidity and consistent moisture are essential; this is one of the most challenging tropical fruits to grow outside its native range.

planting.note: Plant in frost-free conditions with well-drained soil; black sapote is more drought-tolerant than many tropical fruits once established and tolerates mild neglect in zone 10 conditions.

directions: Plant in full sun in well-drained, moderately fertile soil in frost-free conditions; black sapote produces tomato-sized fruits with thin green skin that turns olive-yellow at full ripeness, concealing soft black pulp with a rich, sweet, chocolate-like flavor that famously resembles chocolate pudding — often eaten directly from the skin with a spoon. A relative of persimmons, it must be completely soft before eating; hard fruits are extremely astringent. Excellent used in smoothies, ice cream, mousses, and as a low-fat substitute for chocolate in baked goods. Trees are moderately fast-growing and begin bearing 3–5 years from transplant; an outstanding conversation piece and genuinely delicious tropical fruit for zone 10–12 gardens.

common_name: Water Caltrop (Bat Nut, Water Chestnut of Europe)

planting.note: Drop nuts into still, shallow water (1–6 feet deep) after last frost; water caltrop is invasive in many US states and banned in several — check local regulations before planting; grow only in contained garden ponds where escape into natural waterways is prevented.

directions: Place fresh nuts in shallow, still water in full sun after last frost; water caltrop is a floating aquatic annual with attractive diamond-shaped floating leaves and distinctive 4-horned nut fruits (resembling a bat or medieval caltrop weapon) that have been consumed in Asia and Europe since the Neolithic period. The large starchy nuts are boiled, roasted, or eaten raw and have a pleasant chestnut-like flavor; they are still commonly sold in Chinese, Japanese, and South Asian markets. Listed as invasive in many US states due to rapid spread and choking of natural waterways — only cultivate in well-contained, sealed garden ponds from which fruits are thoroughly collected to prevent seed escape. Historically important in China, where it is among the most ancient cultivated aquatic crops.
